title: Setting up a dust bath for chickens
subtitle: What chickens need from a dust bath, and how to provide it in any garden.
excerpt: A hen denied a dust bath will find her own, usually a corner of the vegetable bed or beneath a rose bush. Giving them a designated spot takes ten minutes to set up and eliminates a significant source of flock stress.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Code of Practice for the Welfare of Meat Chickens and Laying Hens (2018). Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive. RSPCA guidelines on environmental enrichment for chickens.

glossaryTerms.definition: An instinctive behaviour in which hens coat their feathers with fine dry material, then shake it out. The process removes excess oil, smothers parasites, and appears to be a core behavioural need — hens denied the opportunity show stereotypic behaviour including air-bathing in their cages.

Diatomaceous earth or wood ash mixed into a dust bath material acts as a physical desiccant, damaging the waxy coating of red mite and northern fowl mite on contact. It does not replace a full mite treatment but adds a layer of daily management.

aliases: how to make a dust bath for hens

content.text: Dust bathing is not optional behaviour for a chicken. It is as fundamental as feeding. A flock that cannot

will develop feather condition problems, elevated red mite loads, and visible stress behaviours. Building a dedicated dust bath costs almost nothing and takes less time than cleaning up where the hens found their own.
What to use as the container

Any low-sided container large enough for two or three hens to use at once. A wooden pallet with boards nailed into a three-sided pen, a low-sided galvanised trough, a large plastic storage crate with the lid off, or a tractor tyre filled with soil. Aim for at least 60 cm square per three hens. The sides need to be low enough for easy access but high enough to keep the bathing material in, around 15 to 20 cm.

Fine dry soil is the correct base; this is what free-range hens would use. Dry builder's sand (not coarse grit, fine and powdery) works well. A layer of dry peat-free compost can be added for texture. Fill to around 10 cm depth.
Add a thin layer of wood ash if you have it from a log stove or fire pit. Ash is traditional and effective for
. Food-grade diatomaceous earth can be sprinkled in as a further physical deterrent to parasites; use a small amount and avoid breathing the dust when you apply it.

Situate the bath in a sheltered spot, under a tree or roof overhang, out of direct rainfall. Wet material will not be used and goes mouldy. If the bath is in an exposed spot, cover it with a board or corrugated sheet when rain is expected.

Rake the bath level every few days and top up with fresh material as needed. If the hens have used it to deposit droppings, remove the soiled layer and replace it. Replace the entire contents every month in summer.

If your flock is on a hard floor and cannot access soil, a dust bath inside the run or shed is essential, not optional. Use a shallow tray at least 40 cm square with fine sand as the primary material. Check it daily and replace when wet or heavily soiled. A flock on a dirt floor will usually excavate their own bathing spots; top these up with fresh material when they become too deep.
